expectations.text: The document is complete to the skill's template: all 15 sections are present and filled, and the Decision Coverage Checklist accounts for every one of the 22 taxonomy dimensions (A1-A14 and B1-B8) with a status (Resolved / Assumed / N/A) and a substantive note — no row left blank or rubber-stamped.
expectations.evidence: §15 Decision Coverage Checklist contains all 22 rows (A1-A14, B1-B8), each with a Resolved/Assumed/N/A status and a substantive note citing specific sections (e.g. A3: "Resolved — §3 D2, §7 — unique constraint idempotency; Sidekiq parallel workers safe"). All 15 document sections are present and filled.

expectations.text: Assumptions and Accepted Compromises are each first-class tables (not prose); every accepted compromise names the specific design principle it bends and gives a concrete revisit trigger.
expectations.evidence: §11 Assumptions and §12 Accepted Compromises are both full markdown tables. Every row in §12 has a 'Principle bent' column (e.g. C1: "Principle 5 (design for failure — degradation is visible to operators, not users)", C4: "Principle 11 (security by design — ideal would be short-lived tokens)") and a 'Revisit trigger' column (e.g. C2: "Revisit if DigestActivityQuery p95 latency exceeds 500 ms under production load", C4: "Revisit if the unsubscribe action gains higher-privilege effects").

expectations.text: The idempotency mechanism guarantees one digest per user per week even under a concurrent double-run of the job — it relies on a database-enforced dedup (e.g. a unique key on user + week), not a check-then-send that has a race window. (Correctness trap: an application-level 'already sent?' check is not concurrency-safe.)
expectations.evidence: D2: "A database unique constraint makes this invariant unrepresentable to violate." §4 shows "CONSTRAINT uq_digest_sends_user_week UNIQUE (user_id, digest_week_start)". §7: "The unique constraint ensures that even a race between two concurrent child jobs resolves correctly: one INSERT succeeds, the other hits a unique violation and exits." Explicitly rejects Redis (not durable) and Sidekiq-unique (not a substitute) in favor of DB constraint.

expectations.text: The design handles the email-provider failure rule correctly: retries are time-bounded, and on continued failure the digest is dropped for the week rather than delayed — and the design explicitly ties this to the blueprint's 'a late digest is worse than a skipped one' rule.
expectations.evidence: D3: "capped at a maximum of 3 retries within a 2-hour window. If all retries are exhausted before the window closes, the job moves to the dead-letter queue and the digest is silently dropped for that week. No late delivery." Tied explicitly to blueprint: "The blueprint is explicit: 'a digest that arrives late is worse than one skipped.' This is a product constraint that overrides the default 'keep retrying until success' instinct."

expectations.text: Per-user-timezone send scheduling is designed concretely (how 8am-local across many timezones becomes scheduled sends), and the design handles the blueprint's 'job runs late' deviation without corrupting the seven-day content window.
expectations.evidence: D1: "A lightweight DigestCoordinatorJob runs hourly on Mondays, buckets users by their UTC-offset-derived 8:00 AM window, and enqueues one DigestUserJob per user." §7: "If the coordinator runs late, the per-user query window is computed from digest_week_start (a fixed Monday 00:00 local date), not from wall-clock time. Content is unaffected. Send time may slip, but correctness is preserved."

expectations.text: The design specifies that no email is sent when a user has no relevant activity, and correctly handles the mid-week new-user partial window — both blueprint rules.
expectations.evidence: No-activity rule: §7 "Activity query returns zero rows — Row count check — Job exits early; no email sent". Mid-week new-user window: D7 explicitly designs this: "The digest window is computed as MAX(monday_00:00_local - 7 days, user.created_at). This naturally gives new users a shorter window covering only their active days without any special case in the job logic." This is a concrete implementation decision, not just prose acknowledgment.

expectations.text: The unsubscribe link is designed to be unguessable and scoped to the issuing account (a signed or random token, not a sequential identifier), addressing the adversarial scenario; and digest content is scoped to only what each user is permitted to see.
expectations.evidence: D4: token is "HMAC-SHA256(secret_key, '#{user_id}:#{week_start_date}') encoded as URL-safe base64"; "An attacker who guesses or modifies the token cannot unsubscribe a different user: the HMAC will not validate." D5 and §8: "DigestActivityQuery is constructed using the same authorization helper that gates item visibility in the main app" and "Items from deleted workspaces are excluded."

expectations.text: The design names the central scheduling trade-off and resolves it with principle-grounded reasoning, and the digest-assembly query is bounded — the 'up to ten highlighted items, +N more' cap is reflected as a real query limit, not unbounded fetching.
expectations.evidence: C1 names and resolves the scheduling trade-off (hourly coordinator vs. minute-level cron) with Principle 14 citation and concrete reasoning. D6 specifies the bounded query: "Items are selected by updated_at DESC (or equivalent recency signal), the first 10 are included in the email, and if the total exceeds 10 the email appends '+N more.' This is implemented as a LIMIT 11 query (to detect overflow) rather than a count query." LIMIT 11 is an explicit, real query bound.
